title: About dl-lstm-forecast
tagline: How an LSTM, 883 days of NYC bike-share data, and MC-Dropout uncertainty turn a date pick into a 14-day forecast with a confidence band.

sections.title: The Problem
body: How many bikes will Citi Bike riders take out tomorrow? Next Saturday? Two weeks from today? Daily ride counts are a textbook time-series problem with three signals layered on top of each other — a strong weekly cycle (commute peaks Mon–Fri, leisure peaks on weekends), a yearly seasonal cycle (summer ridership dwarfs winter), and an overall growth trend as the bike-share system expanded.
The forecasting task: given a window of recent daily ride counts, predict the next 14 days with a confidence band that honestly reflects how much the model trusts its own guess.

sections.title: Approach — Why an LSTM?
body: A linear regression on lagged values handles the weekly cycle but misses the trend. A SARIMA model handles both but tunes brittlely on real data. A **Long Short-Term Memory** network learns *which lags to attend to* automatically — its hidden state acts as a learned summary of the input window, and the gating mechanism decides what to keep and what to discard across timesteps.
Even with just 64 hidden units and one layer (~17K parameters), this LSTM learns the weekly periodicity from the input window without anyone telling it that the period is 7 days.

bullets.label: Why a single layer
bullets.text: Daily counts have ~700 training days — stacked LSTMs would over-fit. One layer with hidden=64 is the standard tutorial recipe that converges quickly and generalizes well.

bullets.label: Why dropout = 0.2
bullets.text: Modest regularization, but more importantly it's the source of **MC Dropout** uncertainty estimates at inference (see Performance section).

bullets.label: Why MSE loss
bullets.text: Penalizes large misses quadratically — appropriate when the target is a continuous count with no boundaries.

bullets.label: Why 28-day window
bullets.text: Four full weekly cycles. Long enough to capture the weekly + recent-trend signal, short enough to keep training fast.

sections.title: How 1-step + Autoregressive Rollout Gives a 14-Day Forecast
body: The LSTM is trained to predict **one** day ahead. To produce a 14-day forecast, the model is rolled forward autoregressively: the prediction for day t+1 is fed back as the last value of the input window, then the model predicts day t+2, and so on for 14 steps.

This is honest about uncertainty: errors compound, and the confidence band visibly widens with horizon. A model trained directly to predict 14 days ahead would over-smooth and lose the daily-level structure.

diagram.code:
graph LR
  W["Window: last 28 days"] --> L1["LSTM"] --> P1["ŷ(t+1)"]
  P1 -.->|"shift window"| W2["Window: last 27 + ŷ(t+1)"]
  W2 --> L2["LSTM"] --> P2["ŷ(t+2)"]
  P2 -.-> Dots["... × 12 more steps ..."]
  Dots -.-> P14["ŷ(t+14)"]

  classDef inout fill:#0d2818,stroke:#22c55e,color:#86efac
  classDef step fill:#13161e,stroke:#4f8ef7,color:#e2e8f0
  class W,W2 inout
  class L1,L2,P1,P2,P14,Dots step

sections.title: How the Confidence Band Works (MC Dropout)
body: **Monte Carlo Dropout** treats the trained LSTM as a Bayesian neural network at inference time. Normally dropout is disabled when you call `.eval()` on a PyTorch model — but if you leave it active, each forward pass becomes a stochastic sample from an implicit posterior over the model's weights.

For every step of the autoregressive rollout, the model is queried **N=30 times** with dropout active. The mean of those 30 samples is the forecast; the 2σ envelope is the confidence band. The band grows with horizon because forecast errors compound — uncertainty made visible without needing to train an ensemble of separate models.

bullets.label: Why dropout-only enable
bullets.text: BatchNorm and other train-only layers stay in eval mode at inference (running statistics shouldn't drift). Only Dropout flips back on — there's a helper `enable_mc_dropout(model)` that does exactly this.

bullets.label: Why N=30 samples
bullets.text: Enough to get a stable variance estimate per step; 30 × 14 steps = 420 forward passes through a tiny LSTM ≈ 1.5s on NAS CPU, comfortably under Cloudflare's 100s origin-response ceiling.

bullets.label: What 'lower' / 'upper' mean
bullets.text: mean − 2σ and mean + 2σ — the 95% credible interval under the Gaussian assumption.

sections.title: Model Architecture
body: One LSTM layer + one linear head. ~17K trainable parameters.

sections.title: Service Architecture
body: Same per-project 4-layer pattern (ADR-013) used by every project on pandyaHomeLab. The browser sends an anchor date; the server returns a JSON payload of 14 forecast points (mean / lower / upper / optional actual). The chart on the page is pure Chart.js — no framework, no build step.

sections.title: Network & Deployment
body: The DL domain has its own bridge network (ADR-016) — dl-postgres, dl-minio, dl-redis, and dl-mlflow live there alongside this demo and the sibling dl-mnist-cnn. The MLflow tracker is exposed on its own subdomain (`mlflow-dl.pandyahomelab.com`) per the platform's locked subdomain pattern for per-domain MLflow.
No cross-network reach-back into ml-network — domain autonomy means each domain's tracking, storage, and demos are independent of every other domain's.

sections.title: Dataset
body: NYC CitiBike daily ride counts, sourced from [toddwschneider/nyc-citibike-data](https://github.com/toddwschneider/nyc-citibike-data) — a well-known reference repo that publishes a daily-aggregated CSV of trip counts. **883 daily observations** spanning 2013-07-01 → 2015-11-30, the early-growth era of the CitiBike system.

Each row is one calendar day with the total number of bike trips across all stations. The committed CSV also includes weather/holiday columns (precipitation, temperature, dow, holiday) — v1.0.0 uses only the daily trips count, but those features are preserved on disk for a future multivariate extension.

sections.title: Read the Code Walkthrough
body: For a guided tour of how this service is built — file by file, layer by layer — start with the **ML Project Walkthrough** (uses iris-knn as the worked example but the 4-layer architecture is identical). A DL-specific walkthrough covering LSTM training, MC Dropout, and autoregressive forecasting is planned once 2c ships.

bullets.label: Project anatomy
bullets.text: Annotated file tree — what every file is and why it's there.

bullets.label: Request flow
bullets.text: A `/forecast` request traced hop-by-hop from the browser through all four layers.

bullets.label: Training flow
bullets.text: What happens during eager warm-up at container startup — load CSV → fit scaler → train LSTM with early stopping → evaluate → log to dl-mlflow.

bullets.label: Per-layer patterns
bullets.text: Factory pattern, lazy init, thread-safe train lock, `__file__`-relative paths, the hyphen-vs-underscore symlink trick.

bullets.label: MLflow integration
bullets.text: How runs are logged to the per-domain tracker; graceful degradation when the tracker is briefly unreachable.

sections.title: Performance
sections.problem_type: Univariate time-series regression · single target (daily trips) · last 180 days held out
body: Trained on roughly 613 days (everything before the final 270 days), validated on the next 90 days for early stopping, and evaluated on the final 180 days. Adam (lr=1e-3) for up to 50 epochs with patience-5 early stopping — in practice the model usually stops around epoch 15–25 once the val loss plateaus.

Because the target is a continuous count, the natural summary metrics are **MAPE** (mean absolute percentage error) and **RMSE** (root mean squared error in raw rides/day). 99%-style accuracy targets that work for MNIST don't apply here — the test window straddles a period of fast CitiBike growth, and the model sees only the lagged trip counts, with no weather or holiday features that drive much of the daily variance.

metrics_detail.name: Test MAPE
metrics_detail.what: Mean Absolute Percentage Error — average size of the model's relative miss across the 180-day test window.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Scale-free — interpretable as 'on average the forecast is off by X%'. Easy to compare across forecasting tasks with different units.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Sensitive to days with unusually low ride counts (a 1000-ride absolute miss looks worse on a 5,000-ride day than on a 30,000-ride day). MAPE in the 20%+ range here is honest for univariate LSTM on noisy daily counts without weather features.

metrics_detail.name: Test RMSE
metrics_detail.what: Root Mean Squared Error — typical absolute error in rides/day.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: In the same units as the target (rides/day) — useful for grounding the percentage error in something tangible: 'on a typical day, the forecast is off by ~Y rides'.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Penalises large misses quadratically, so a few extreme weather days can dominate the average. Always read RMSE alongside MAPE.

metrics_detail.name: Best val loss
metrics_detail.what: MSE on the validation window (90 days immediately before the test set) in scaled space.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Drives the early-stopping decision — training halts when this stops improving for `patience` epochs.
metrics_detail.watch_out: In scaled space (mean 0, std 1 on train), so a value of 0.2–0.3 is typical for this dataset. Not directly comparable to RMSE in rides/day.
